metadata.title: Confusion Matrix Audit: 95% Accuracy That Learns Nothing

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Watching the dummy classifier hit 95% by always predicting negative made accuracy feel like a lie under class imbalance. I want to build and break the same audit myself. Help me: (1) write the code to generate 9,500 negatives and 500 positives and train both a dummy and a logistic model; (2) compute precision, recall, and F1 side by side and explain why the dummy's recall is zero while accuracy stays high; (3) recommend which single metric I should gate deployment on for a rare-disease screen.

props.command: Watching the dummy classifier hit 95% by always predicting negative made accuracy feel like a lie under class imbalance. I want to build and break the same audit myself. Help me: (1) write the code to generate 9,500 negatives and 500 positives and train both a dummy and a logistic model; (2) compute precision, recall, and F1 side by side and explain why the dummy's recall is zero while accuracy stays high; (3) recommend which single metric I should gate deployment on for a rare-disease screen.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: A model achieves ninety-five percent accuracy on a disease screening task. The team ships it. A post-deployment audit shows the model catches zero cases — it predicts negative for every patient, exploiting the class imbalance in the test set. Accuracy, on its own, is a misleading metric when one class dominates. The confusion matrix is the audit that exposes this: it shows not what fraction the model got right, but what it actually did with each class. This video builds the audit that catches a trivial model masquerading as a working one.
beats.visual_intent: title card: '95% accuracy — 0% recall'; PROBLEM beat, no terminal

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to generate imbalanced binary data with ninety-five hundred negatives and five hundred positives. Train both a logistic regression and a dummy classifier set to always predict the majority class. Compute confusion matrices, accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 for both. Label which is trivial.

props.command:
claude "Write Python using sklearn:
(1) generate imbalanced binary data:
    9500 negatives, 500 positives, 20 features
(2) train LogisticRegression AND
    DummyClassifier(strategy='most_frequent')
(3) confusion matrices, accuracy, precision,
    recall, F1 for both
(4) label which is 'trivial'
Show 95% accuracy is meaningless here."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — read the code before running
beats.narration_text: Read the key line. Dummy classifier accuracy equals ninety-five hundred divided by ten thousand — ninety-five percent — by predicting negative always. One line. No learning. The code makes this explicit so you can see it before the numbers confirm it.

props.code:
# confusion_audit.py  —  Claude Code output
from sklearn.dummy import DummyClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, classification_report

# DummyClassifier(strategy='most_frequent'):
# Always predicts 'negative' (the majority class)
# => Accuracy = 9500/10000 = 95.0%  (trivial!)
dummy_acc = 9500 / 10000  # <- the trap

# The confusion matrix exposes it:
# TN=9500  FP=0
# FN=500   TP=0   <- catches ZERO positives

# Recall = TP / (TP + FN) = 0 / 500 = 0.0%
# F1 = 0.0%  (harmonic mean with precision=undefined)

beats.narration_text: Run it. Side by side: dummy classifier on the left, logistic regression on the right. The dummy matrix is almost entirely one green cell — ninety-five hundred true negatives — with a large crimson false negative block showing five hundred missed positives. True positive cell: zero. Recall: zero percent. The logistic regression gets three hundred fifty true positives. Recall: seventy percent. Same ninety-five percent accuracy number. Opposite clinical value.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_ConfusionMatrices: two 2x2 confusion matrices side by side — Dummy (TN=9500, FN=500, TP=0, FP=0) vs LogReg (TN=9200, FP=300, FN=150, TP=350); accuracy labels same; recall labels diverge dramatically

beats.narration_text: Accuracy is not the right metric. Ask Claude to update the script to optimize threshold on the logistic regression probability output, sweeping from point-one to point-nine, and find the threshold that maximizes F1 score on the minority class. Show the confusion matrix at that threshold.

props.command:
claude "Update confusion_audit.py:
-> sweep classification threshold 0.1 to 0.9
-> for LogisticRegression probabilities
-> find threshold maximizing F1 on minority class
-> print confusion matrix at optimal threshold
-> compare to default threshold=0.5"

beats.narration_text: At threshold point-three, F1 on the minority class improves and recall rises to eighty-two percent. Accuracy drops to ninety-two. That drop is meaningful: it represents cases you are now catching that the default threshold missed. The lesson: accuracy on imbalanced data is a vanity metric. The confusion matrix tells you who the model is actually failing, and the threshold is where you make the clinical trade-off explicit.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: threshold sweep result; 'accuracy hides imbalance — recall exposes it'

beats.narration_text: Your move: check the class distribution in every dataset you are evaluating on. If the minority class is under ten percent, accuracy is not a valid primary metric. Run the confusion matrix. Compute recall and F1. Sweep the threshold. Document the clinical operating point before shipping any number to a stakeholder.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) check class imbalance, 2) use recall/F1 as primary metrics, 3) sweep threshold and document operating point

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Your move: check the class distribution in every dataset you are evaluating on. If the minority class is under ten percent, accuracy is not a valid primary metric. Run the confusion matrix. Compute recall and F1. Sweep the threshold. Document the clinical operating point before shipping any number to a stakeholder.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Confusion Matrix Audit: 95% Accuracy That Learns Nothing] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
